topic: A college student discovers their roommate has been reading their journal
raw_output:
[story 1]

The morning sun felt like a physical weight pressing down on Alex. Every muscle protested the mere act of rising from the bed. He managed to drag himself upright, the sheets clinging uncomfortably to his skin. As he shuffled toward the bathroom, he noticed something amiss. His worn leather-bound journal, usually tucked under his pillow, was sitting open on his desk, the pages filled with his own rambling thoughts, underlined in a meticulous script that wasn't his. A slow, dull ache settled in his chest. He glanced over at Mark, who was already up, sipping coffee with an air of effortless productivity. Mark didn't look up, but Alex caught the brief flicker of something—a knowing, almost smug expression—before Mark turned back to his screen. Alex just blinked, the effort of processing the intrusion making his eyelids heavy.

[story 2]

It was a Tuesday, and the inertia of the day was thick enough to chew. Sarah felt profoundly unmotivated, the urge to stay under the duvet an almost irresistible gravitational pull. When she finally hauled herself into the kitchen for a lukewarm instant ramen, she paused by her desk. Her personal diary, a collection of half-formed ideas and late-night anxieties, was open. The ink on the page was sharp, deliberate, and undeniably hers. A strange, prickly sensation crawled up her neck. She didn't want to confront anyone, especially not when her energy reserves were critically low. She just stared, the silence of the room amplifying the sudden, sharp awareness of being observed, a feeling that made her want to sink back into the soft cushions.

[story 3]

Liam was operating on fumes. The concept of making breakfast seemed like an insurmountable obstacle, a monumental effort requiring too much coordination. He slumped onto his chair, letting his head fall back against the cool wood. Later, while attempting to find a misplaced textbook, he spotted his journal. It was open, the handwriting—his own looping, messy script—being meticulously scrutinized by his roommate. A sudden, jarring spike of irritation shot through him, quickly followed by a deep sense of resignation. He didn't move to correct the situation. Instead, he just let his shoulders drop further, a silent acknowledgment that engaging with the reality of the situation felt like too much exertion for this particular moment.
